observations.summary: Father Aviel's cessation of inner life after Mother's death parallels the theme of spiritual decay and darkness described in Chapter 7's depiction of the Pit, suggesting a motif of halted vitality and deep corruption.

source_evidence.quote: But when Mother Zuna passed beyond recall—when they buried his bride beneath the earth—something stopped within him. Not died, which would have been cleaner and quicker, but simply ceased like a cart with a cruelly shattered axle.

observations.target_hint: The description of the Pit's deep corruption and spiritual sickness in Chapter 7, evoking a similar sense of halted life and decay.

target_evidence.quote: The smell of decay got stronger, becoming too much, a thick, sickening smell of spoiled things, of rot given shape, of the most terrible, gross, awful, rotting, foul filth. It was the smell of the Pit's deep corruption, a stench of spiritual sickness.
